channel.title: Math on jgoldfar's home on the web
channel.description: Recent content in Math on jgoldfar's home on the web

item.title: Interpolating Data in a Vector Space
item.description: I’d like to show in this note that Julia’s “official” interpolation package, Interpolations.jl can work natively with any type of object that allows some vector space operations over the field of real (or floating point) numbers. That is, in order to interpolate between two values y_1 and y_2, those values must have compatible, closed scalar multiplication and vector addition operations. First, some preamble (to make sure we can load Interpolations in this workspace)

item.title: Mathematical Biology Numerics Workshop
item.description: Below is the output from running this notebook on Google Colab; you’ll need to select “open in playground” to make changes or run cells. We will consider the solution of the problem \begin{align} \dot{x}(t) = f(t, x, u), \end{align} \begin{align} x(t_0) = x^0 \in \mathbb{R}^n \end{align} where \begin{align} x(t) = \big( x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)\big) \end{align} is the state vector, \begin{align} u = \big( u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_m\big) \in \mathbb{R}^m \end{align} is the parameter vector, and \begin{align} f(t,x,u) = \big( f_1(t, x, u), f_2(t, x, u),\ldots, f_n(t, x, u)\big): [t_0, T] \times \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^m \to \mathbb{R}^n \end{align}

item.title: A Brief Introduction to the Implicit Function Theorem
item.description: Motivating Example Consider a curve such as that defining a unit circle in the plane, \[x^{2} + y^{2}=1\] Note that this definition is a special case of a more general situation, a curve defined as a level set of a function \[f(x,y):= x^{2} + y^{2}= 1 =:z_{0}\] Since this is a relatively simple curve, we can draw it and observe that, while it is not a function with respect to either \(x\) or \(y\) (that is, in general we cannot solve the equation for either \(y\) or \(x\), respectively), in a small neighborhood of any point (signified by a dashed circle in Figure 1 about the given point) the curve is indeed a function, as we can see:
